coredata.dc:title: A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated Z-scheme g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots heterostructure photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yingying
Huang, Qunzeng
Wang, Jianshe
He, Zhanhang
Li, Zhongjun
coredata.dc:description: A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots (NCDS) heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by thermal polymerization and subsequent solvothermal approach. Under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) irradiation, the as-prepared g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite with 3wt% of MoS2 and 2wt% of NCDS displayed the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity with a corresponding H2 evolution rate of 212.41μmol g−1 h−1, which was about 53 times higher than that of g-C3N4/MoS2-3%. Moreover, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite exhibited excellent photostability. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite was predominantly attributed to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and NCDS, which effectively accelerated the transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Besides, the introduction of noble metal-free MoS2 co-catalyst further promoted the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and provided more active sites for H2 evolution reaction. The advantages of superior photocatalytic activity and excellent stability endowed a promising potential for g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite to apply in photocatalytic fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
N-doped carbon dots
MoS2QDs
Z-scheme heterojunction
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCDS and bulk g-C3N4/NCDS photocatalyst
Preparation of protonated g-C3N4/NCDS photocatalyst and protonated g-C3N4
Preparation of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 and g-C3N4/MoS2 photocatalyst
BET surface areas and pore size distributions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2.
The zeta potentials of bulk g-C3N4, bulk g-C3N4/NCDS, protonated g-C3N4/NCDS, and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 photocatalysts.
TEM and HRTEM images of (a) NCDS and (b)MoS2 QDs; (c–d) g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%; (e) STEM-EDX image of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite and the corresponding element mapping for C, N, Mo, and S.
The XPS spectra of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite: (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) Mo 3d, and (d) S 2p.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption desorption curve and (b) Pore size distribution curve of bulk g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% complexes.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) the plots of (αhν)2 versus band-gap energy (hν) of bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 nanosheet samples.
FT-IR spectra of NCDS, bulk g-C3N4, protonated g-C3N4/NCDS, and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%.

(a) The rate of H2 production over g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 photocatalysts loaded with 3% MoS2 and various amounts of NCDS under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). (b) Photocatalytic reaction of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite photocatalyst in visible Light (λ ≥ 420 nm) for 3 h. (A: bulk g-C3N4. B: g-C3N4/MoS2-3%. C: g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-2%. D: g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%. E: g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-4%. F: g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-5%).
Photocatalytic hydrogen production over (g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%) during 16 h, with evacuation every 4 h.
The PL spectra of the as-prepared samples.
Transient photocurrent density versus time plotted (a) and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of bulk g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3%, and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% under visible light (λ > 400 nm) in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for hydrogen evolution over g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 under visible light irradiation.
Summary of specific surface area and pore size of bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%.

head.ce:title: A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated Z-scheme g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots heterostructure photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Engineering Technology Research Center of Henan Province for Solar Catalysis, Nanyang Normal University, Nanyang 473061, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Engineering Technology Research Center of Henan Province for Solar Catalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) decorated g-C3N4 nanosheet/N-doped carbon dots (NCDS) heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by thermal polymerization and subsequent solvothermal approach. Under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) irradiation, the as-prepared g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite with 3wt% of MoS2 and 2wt% of NCDS displayed the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity with a corresponding H2 evolution rate of 212.41μmol g−1 h−1, which was about 53 times higher than that of g-C3N4/MoS2-3%. Moreover, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite exhibited excellent photostability. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite was predominantly attributed to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and NCDS, which effectively accelerated the transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Besides, the introduction of noble metal-free MoS2 co-catalyst further promoted the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and provided more active sites for H2 evolution reaction. The advantages of superior photocatalytic activity and excellent stability endowed a promising potential for g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite to apply in photocatalytic fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Antonietti et al. initially reported that graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was active for water splitting, it has attracted tremendous attentions due to its relatively narrow band gap, proper band edges, cost effectiveness and excellent chemical stability [1–3]. However, the high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers constrains its photocatalytic activity and wide application [4–6]. To address this issue, different strategies have been developed, including element doping [7], heterojunction constructing [8] and nanostructure controlling [9,10]. Among these strategies, constructing heterojunction is considered to be one of the most effective ways. So far, various heterojunctions have been constructed, such as semiconductor/g-C3N4 junction (TiO2/g-C3N4 [11], BiOBr/g-C3N4 [12], ZnS/g-C3N4 [13], CoP/g-C3N4 [14]), polymer/g-C3N4 junction (C3N4-PEDOT-Pt [15]), carbon/g-C3N4 junction (carbon dots/g-C3N4 [16,17], carbon/g-C3N4 nanofibers [18], carbon framework/g-C3N4 [19], carbon ring/g-C3N4 [20]). Among them, carbon dots/g-C3N4 heterojunction, due to the characteristic of carbon dots such as chemical stability, low cost, unique light induced electron transfer and electron storage properties, is regarded as one of the most promising materials for water splitting [21]. Although the performance of carbon dots/g-C3N4 is impressive, the most disadvantage is the involving of noble metals such as Pt [22], Pd and Au [23]. Therefore, it is of great significant to develop g-C3N4 based composite photocatalysts with high performance without using noble metals.

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has unique structure with the unsaturated atoms of both Mo and S at the edges, which can provide favorable edge activity during the catalytic process. Recent researches have shown that MoS2 can be used as an effective co-catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [24–32]. For instance, Zhao et al. [33] and Hou et al. [24] reported g-C3N4/MoS2 and g-C3N4/graphene/MoS2 composite photocatalysts and investigated their photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity, respectively. Wang et al. reported self-optimization of the active site of molybdenum disulfide by an irreversible phase transition during photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [34]. Up to now, most studies are confined to MoS2 nanosheet co-catalyst. Compared with monolayered MoS2, more unsaturated terminal sulfur atoms can serve as the active site in MoS2 nanodots [35]. Therefore, MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) appears to be a more favorable candidate as the co-catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

In this work, We presented the construction and characterization of ternary g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2QDs photocatalysts and their visible-light induced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity. The effects of NCDS and MoS2QDs contents on H2 evolution rate of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 QDs were studied. The results indicated that both NCDS and MoS2 were crucial to increasing the photocatalytic activity. The prepared ternary g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 QDs composites showed remarkably enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activities compared to g-C3N4/NCDS and g-C3N4/MoS2. Based on the experimental results, proper mechanisms were proposed to account for the improved photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. To the best of our knowledge, the ternary g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 photocatalyst has not been reported yet. Therefore, this work presents a novel photocatalyst system for the potential application in photocatalytic fields.

Dicyandiamide (C2H4N4, 99%), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Urea, Citric acid, Ammonium tetrathiomolybdate ((NH4)2MoS4), hydrochloric acid, were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used without further purification.

The synthesis of bulk g-C3N4 was based on a published method [36,37]. Typically, 5.0 g of dicyandiamide was introduced into an alumina crucible with a cover which was heated to 550℃for 3 h with a heating rate of 3℃/min. The obtained bulk g-C3N4 was ground into powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDS and bulk g-C3N4/NCDS photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped carbon dots (NCDS) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method [36]. Detailed experimental process was listed in the supporting information. The preparation process of bulk g-C3N4/NCDS was as follows: a certain quantity of NCDS was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water. Then, 3.0 g of dicyandiamide was added into above solution. The mixture was mechanically stirred at 70 °C to remove the solvent. The obtained powder was subsequently heated to 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 3 °C/min. After being cooled to room temperature, the resultant yellow powder was collected. The obtained sample was denoted as bulk g-C3N4/NCDS-x. x represented the mass fraction of NCDS contained in the composite. Different bulk g-C3N4/NCDS samples with NCDS at mass percentages of 1.0%,1.5%, 2.0%, 2.5%, and 3.0% were prepared through the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of protonated g-C3N4/NCDS photocatalyst and protonated g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 g of bulk g-C3N4/NCDS-x was dispersed into 50 mL of 6 M HCl solution, the obtained mixture was transfered to a teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 130 °C for 5 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the photocatalyst was obtained by centrifugation-oscillation-ultrasonic cycle several times and vacuum drying at 80 °C for 5 h. The obtained photocatalyst was denoted as protonated g-C3N4/NCDS-x. Protonated g-C3N4 was prepared in the same manner as protonated g-C3N4/NCDS except that bulk g-C3N4/NCDS-x was replaced with bulk g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 and g-C3N4/MoS2 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: A quantity of (NH4)2MoS4 was dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 60 mg of protonated g-C3N4/NCDS-2.0% was mixed with the above solution, and then sonicated for 1 h. The above mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 200 °C for 24 h. The product was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with deionized water to completely remove DMF. The obtained product was dried at 70 °C overnight and denoted as g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-x, x represented the weight percentage of MoS2 in the composite. g-C3N4/MoS2 was prepared in the same manner as g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 except that the protonated g-C3N4/NCDS was replaced with protonated g-C3N4.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an Empyrean multipurpose research diffractometer (Panalytical Empyrean XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Transition electron microscopy (TEM) images were recored with a JEOL 2100 TEM microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and valence band XPS (VB XPS) were obtained by using Thermo Escalab 150 spectrometer with an Al Kα X-ray. The fluorescence properties of the samples were measured using a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer (model F-4600), which is the excitation source. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was obtained at 77 K on a Quantachrome specific surface and aperture meter (NOVA 1000e). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were constructed on an Agilent Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer equipped. FTIR spectra were performed with a Nicolet Nexus 470 spectrometer.

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity was evaluated using CEL-SPH2N photolysis water production system (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co., Ltd). A self-made glass vessel was used as a photocatalytic reactor (reactor volume of 500 mL). Prior to performing the photocatalytic reaction, a vacuum pump was started to evacuate the reactor and the hydrogen circulation path. During the catalytic reaction, the reaction was maintained at a constant temperature with circulating condensed water, and the system was stirred with a magnetic stirrer to keep it in suspension. 0.05 g photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of water containing 10 vol.% triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent. The suspension was evacuated several times to completely remove air. The light-irradiation was carried out by using a 300 W Xe-lamp (Profect Light, PLS-SXE300C) with a 420 nm cutoff filter as the visible light source. The generated H2 was automatically analyzed every 1 h by a gas chromatographer (GC-7920) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector with high purity N2 as a carrier gas.

Electrochemical test was conducted by using standard three-electrode system, where catalyst as working electrode, platinum plate electrode as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. A Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) was used as the electrolyte, and the light source was a 300 W Xe lamp. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the samples was tested by the AC Impedenance mode of the RST 5200 electrochemical workstation. The instantaneous photocurrent test was performed by using the Amperometric i-t curve mode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed over a frequency range of 103 to 105 Hz with an AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV.

The process for synthesizing the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 . In order to investigate the formation process of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite materials, the surface charge of bulk g-C3N4, bulk g-C3N4/NCDS, protonated g-C3N4/NCDS and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 were measured and the results were given in Fig. 1 . The Z-potential of bulk g-C3N4 and bulk g-C3N4/NCDS was −3.7 mV and −3.5 mV, respectively, indicating that loading NCDS on bulk g-C3N4 did not change the surface charge of bulk g-C3N4. The Z-potential of protonated g-C3N4/NCDS was 4.7 mV, which displayed that the protonation of g-C3N4/NCDS with hydrochloric acid converted the surface charge of g-C3N4/NCDs from negative to positive. Thus, self-assembly formation of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 was achieved by electrostatic adsorption between the negatively charged MoS4 2− and the positively charged g-C3N4/NCDS.

XRD analysis was used to investigate the phase structure of the photocatalysts (Fig. 2 a). The bulk g-C3N4 had two obvious peaks at 13.1° and 27.7°. The lower angle diffraction peak at 13.1° was indexed as tri-s-triazine units (100) reflections, and the one centering at 27.7° could be indexed to the stacking of the conjugated double bonds for the interlayer stacking of aromatic segments (002) [38]. A typical diffraction peak of bulk g-C3N4 could be observed in protonated g-C3N4, which meant that g-C3N4 with a graphite-like layer structure was not changed after protonation. Somewhat differently, the intensity of the (002) peak of protonated g-C3N4 was lower than that of bulk g-C3N4, together with a shift in the (002) peak location from 27.4° for bulk g-C3N4 to 27.6° for g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% (Fig. 2b), descripting a few-layered structure of protonated g-C3N4. In addition, due to the low quantity of MoS2 dispersed on the g-C3N4, no significant peak of MoS2 was found in the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were investigated by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 3 ). The TEM image in Fig. 3a–b displayed the spherical morphology of NCDS and MoS2 QDs without obvious aggregation. TEM images of the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite samples (Fig. 3c) showed that the thin and flaked g-C3N4 was modified with randomly distributed crystalline NCDS and MoS2 QDs. As shown in Fig. 3d, the crystalline lattice distance of 0.53 nm could be corresponded to the (002) plane of MoS2, the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm corresponded to NCDS and the lattice spacing of 0.36 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of g-C3N4. In addition, the HAADF-STEM element mapping of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% samples (Fig. 3e) further demonstrated that MoS2 QDs were well dispersed on g-C3N4 nanosheet. The result showed that all the major elements of C, N, Mo, and S were uniformly distributed in the structure of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample, which further confirmed that MoS2 QDs were deposited on the surface of g-C3N4.

XPS was used to analysis the elemental composition and chemical states of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample (Fig. 4 a, b, c, d). Fig.S1 presented the XPS survey spectrum of the composite, including elements of C, N, O and also a small amount of Mo and S. Typical peaks could be detected at binding energies of 289.8 (C 1 s), 401.4 (N 1 s), 537.6 (O 1 s), 226.4 (Mo 3d). The C 1 s peak in Fig. 4a could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 288.1 eV and 284.6 eV. The strong one at 284.5 eV was corresponding to sp2 carbon bonded to the three nitrogen atoms in the carbon nitride lattice [39–41], and the weak one represented the surface adventitious carbon [42,43]. N 1 s in Fig. 4b had four asymmetrical peaks at 395.0, 396.5, 397.6 and 401.0 eV, respectively, which could be attributed to CNC sp2− bonded N atoms in graphite-like g-C3N4 structure, tertiary (N(C)3) groups, amino functional groups (CNH), and charging effect in heptazine rings [44–46]. Fig. 4c showed the Mo 3d XPS spectrum. The Mo 3d peaks (Fig. 3c) located at 228.7 and 231.7 eV were attributed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 4d presented two obvious peaks at 160.90 and 164.70 eV, corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, demonstrating the existence of S2− state in the photocatalyst [47,48]. The XPS results showed that the MoS2 had been successfully deposited onto the g-C3N4 nanosheet surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface areas and pore size distributions
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of bulk g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 5 . The type of adsorption-desorption isotherms for all samples shown in Fig. 5a belonged to Type IV, indicating that the carbon nitride materials belonged to the mesoporous material. The specific surface area and pore size of bulk g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% are listed in Table 1 . The specific surface area of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% was 71.39 m2/g, which was higher than that of bulk g-C3N4 (25.18 m2/g) and g-C3N4/MoS2-3% (57.04 m2/g). The increased surface area could be attributed to the exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4 in the process of protonation and the loading of MoS2, which facilitate the increase of active sites and thus enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity. From Table 1, it was found that the pore volume of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/MoS2-3% was also increased.

The optical properties of bulk g-C3N4, protonated g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 with different MoS2 quantities were recorded in Fig. 6 a. In comparison with pure g-C3N4, a broader absorption in the visible region was observed for the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% composite. Obviously, the absorption intensity for g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% in the visible region was strengthened as the content of MoS2 increased due to the intrinsic absorption of black-colored MoS2 nanoparticles. Compared with the protonated g-C3N4, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample showed enhanced absorption of the visible-light in the range of 440–800 nm, which covered a large part of the visible spectrum. The enhanced visible-light adsorption ability of the composite could improve the light utilization to generate more available photogenerated electrons for participating in the photocatalytic reaction towards H2 evolution. The plots of (αhν)2 versus band-gap energy (hν) of g-C3N4 samples was given in Fig. 6b. Compared with the bandgap of bulk g-C3N4 (2.82 eV), protonated g-C3N4 had a larger bandgap (2.88 eV), the reason for which was attributed to the well-known quantum confinement effect. The conduction band and valence band potential of g-C3N4 could be calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2) [49].
(1) EVB = χ ‒ Ee + 0.5Eg
(2)
E CB = E VB − Eg

Among them, χ represents the electronegativity of the semiconductor. According to the literature, the electronegativity of g-C3N4 is 4.72 eV [50], and the Ee value is 4.5 eV (vs NHE). Calculated by the above formula, the g-C3N4 nanosheet had a valence band potential of 1.62 eV and a conduction band potential of −1.21 eV.

Fig. S2 (a) and Fig. S2 (b) showed band-gap energy (hν) and Valence-band XPS spectra of NCDS, respectively. The corresponding CB and VB positions of NCDS was calculated based on Eq. (2) were 0.57 eV and 1.87 eV, respectively.

The chemical functional groups of the sample were studied by FTIR spectra given in Fig. 7 . The absorption band at about 808 cm−1 corresponded to the breathing mode of the triazine-units. The bands in the region of 1000–1700 cm−1 were derived from the aromatic CN stretching [51,52], the wider band at 3430 cm-1 was abtribured to COH and NH stretching vibrations [53]. Both pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% had similar absorption bands, which demonstrated that the introduction of NCDS and MoS2 did not change the g-C3N4 layer structure. No MoS2 peak was detected according to the IR spectra. These results were in well consistant with the XRD analysis.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of as-prepared photocatalysts was summarized in Fig. 8 . When no co-catalyst was deposited on the surface of bulk g-C3N4 and protonated g-C3N4/NCDS, both of them had almost no hydrogen evolution performance. After introducing co-catalyst of MoS2, protonated g-C3N4 (g-C3N4/MoS2-3%) exhibited a rather low photocatalytic activity with an average H2 evolution rate of 4.03 μmol g−1 h−1, while the hydrogen evolution activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 can be significantly improved after the incorporation with NCDS. In order to find the optimum content of NCDS, 3% MoS2 was deposited on the surface of protonated g-C3N4/NCDS for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance test (Fig. 8a). The results showed that with the increase of NCDS content, the hydrogen evolution increased gradually. When the content of NCDS reached 2.0%, the hydrogen evolution performance reached optimal. After that, the content of NCDS continued to increase, but the amount of hydrogen evolution decreased. The reason for the decrease could be ascribed to the shielding effect that the excessive black NCDS would partially block the light absorption of g-C3N4.

In addition to NCDS, MoS2 loading had a greater impact on the hydrogen evolution activity of the composite system (Fig. 8b). With the increase of MoS2 loading, the hydrogen evolution activity of the composite system was increased. When the loading of MoS2 was 3 wt%, the composites g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% had the highest photocatalytic performance. The rate of hydrogen evolution was 212.41 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 53 times higher than that of g-C3N4/MoS2-3%. Further increasing the load of MoS2, the hydrogen evolution activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 gradually decreased. The reason for the decrease in hydrogen evolution activity may be related to the following two aspects: first, excessive MoS2 nanoparticles loaded on the protonated g-C3N4/NCDS will have a hiding effect and hinder the absorption of light by the composite photocatalyst; second, overcharged MoS2 will partially become the recombination center of photogenerated carriers, which can inhibit the hydrogen evolution activity of the catalyst. Therefore, the high or low MoS2 loading was unfavorable for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the composite system. The above results indicated that both NCDS and MoS2 played important roles in the photocatalytic process. The rate of H2 production of ternary composite photocatalysts prepared from melamine and urea were also tested, respectively. there is no significant difference in hydrogen production performance (Fig. S3).

The cycling stability of the photocatalyst was also an indispensable index for investigating its photocatalytic effect. To determine the photostability of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3%, the cycling stability of the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample was investigated. The cycling photocatalytic test for g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% under intermittent visible light irradiation was conducted and illustrated in Fig.9 . It was clear that, for four consecutive cycling photocatalytic experiments, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% remained stable without obvious decreasing.

PL analysis was further studied the charge separation and transfer ability of the obtained bulk g-C3N4, protonated g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3% and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample, the result was displayed in Fig.10 . All the samples exhibited broad PL peak centered at 460 nm under the 370 nm excitation at room temperature. Comparing all the spectra, it could be observed that PL intensity obviously decreases with the introduction of MoS2 and NCDS. When 3 wt % MoS2 was loaded on the surface of the g-C3N4/NCDS, the peak intensities drop obviously compared with those of the bulk C3N4 and protonated g-C3N4, demonstrating that the combination of the photoinduced charge carriers was efficiently inhibited by the efficient interfacial charge transfer between the g-C3N4/NCDS and MoS2.

The photogenerated charge separation and electron transfer performance of the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 system were also studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent responses. All samples exhibited a rapid and reproducible photocurrent response at each exposure (Fig. 11 a). It was clear that the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% had a higher photocurrent density than that of bulk g-C3N4, which could be ascribed to the existence of MoS2 and NCDS, where photogenerated electrons and holes could be efficiently separated in space and the photogenerated carrier recombination would be reduced, which kept consistent with the results of PL spectra shown in Fig. 10. As a result, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% sample showed enhanced photocurrent. The improved transfer efficiency of charge carriers could lead to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 photocatalysts. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of bulk g-C3N4, g-C3N4/MoS2-3%, and g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% samples was carried out to further investigate charge transfer and recombination processes in the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composites under the visible light irradiation, and the result was shown in Fig. 11b. Compared with bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/MoS2-3%, the smaller arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2-3% was observed, which indicated a more effective separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and a faster interfacial charge transfer had occurred on the surface. The EIS results were consistent with the photocurrents analysis, which further confirmed that the presence of NCDS and MoS2 was beneficial to separation of electron-hole pairs, thus leading to improved photocatalytic activities.

Based on all the above results, a proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 evolution of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 photocatalyst was illustrated in Fig. 12 . Compared to the CB and VB positions of g-C3N4 nanosheet and NCDS, it could be seen that the E CB of g-C3N4 nanosheet was more negative than that of NCDS and the E VB of NCDS was more positive than that of g-C3N4 nanosheet, as shown in Fig. 12a. It seemed that coupling g-C3N4 nanosheet with NCDS will be form a type-II heterojunction. In this case, photogenerated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 would transfer to NCDS, whereas photogenerated holes in the VB of NCDS would transfer to g-C3N4. However, the accumulated electrons in the CB of NCDS could not reduce water into H2 because the E CB of NCDS was more positive than the H+/H2 redox potential, which was consistent with the fact that g-C3N4/NCDS had almost no hydrogen evolution performance. Thus, the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 will be closer to that of g-C3N4/MoS2. Actually, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite showed highly efficient photocatalytic activities in comparison with g-C3N4/MoS2. Therefore, the enhanced photocatalytic performance should be attributed to the formation of the Z-scheme charge transfer mode (Fig. 12b), which could effectively accelerate the transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Under visible light irradiation, both g-C3N4 and NCDS could be easily excited to yield photogenerated electron–hole pairs. the photogenerated electrons from CB of NCDS would combine rapidly with the photogenerated holes from VB of g-C3N4 nanosheet in the solid-solid interface owing to the short electron-migration distance between NCDS and g-C3N4 nanosheet. So, the rich electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 were further transmitted to the conduction band of MoS2. The CB position of MoS2 located at −0.24 eV [24]. Therefore, compared to MoS2, the more negative CB level of g-C3N4 facilitated to provide ample thermodynamic driving force for the photogenerated electrons transfer from the CB of g-C3N4 to that of MoS2, which also helps to promote the electrons transfer. More importantly, it was revealed that the unsaturated active S atoms on exposed edges of MoS2 could act as the active sites to promote the dissociation of water and the evolution of H2. The photogenerated holes in valence band of NCDS were quickly quenched by TEOA sacrificial agent. As a result, the synergistic effects of Z-scheme charge transfer mode and sufficient active sites for H2 evolution resulted in the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composite.

In summary, the g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 composites had been successfully prepared via an electrostatic self-assembly process followed by a solvothermal process. The protonation of g-C3N4/NCDS not only achieved the transformation of g-C3N4/NCDS from negative charge to positive charge, facilitating the electrostatic self-assembly, but also realized simultaneously the exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4/NCDS, which increased the surface area of composites and provided more active sites for H2 evolution reaction. Ternary g-C3N4/NCDS/MoS2 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance compared with the corresponding two-component systems such as g-C3N4/NCDS, g-C3N4/MoS2. The improvement of photocatalytic activities are attributed to the Z-scheme heterojunction formation between g-C3N4 and NCDS, which facilitates the separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers, enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Current work provided a new strategy for the design and synthesis of high performance composite photocatalysts for water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
